symbols: infant lying in straw — the Word made flesh laid in a manger, humility of the Incarnation
lamb beside the infant — the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world
ox and donkey — Isaiah's prophecy that the ox knows its owner and the donkey its master's crib
Mary's red garment — love, sacrifice, and the blood her son would shed
Joseph kneeling — the faithful guardian bowing before the mystery of the Incarnation
halo around Mary — sanctity and her role as the God-bearer
description: A nativity scene rendered in a muted, almost monochromatic palette with touches of red and brown. The infant Jesus lies on straw at the center, wrapped in white cloth. To the right, Mary kneels in a red and dark garment with a halo, her hands clasped in prayer. To the left, Joseph kneels in white robes, bending toward the child. A donkey lowers its head toward the infant from behind, and a small white lamb lies beside the baby. An ox is partially visible at the right background. The setting is a simple stable interior.
theological_description: Foujita's Nativity presents the birth of Christ with spare, tender intimacy. Luke records: 'And she gave birth to her firstborn son and wrapped him in swaddling cloths and laid him in a manger, because there was no place for them in the inn' (Luke 2:7). The humble stable setting fulfills Micah's prophecy that from Bethlehem, small among the clans of Judah, would come forth one 'whose coming forth is from of old, from ancient days' (Micah 5:2). The lamb lying beside the infant is profoundly symbolic — this child is the Lamb of God to whom John the Baptist would later point: 'Behold, the Lamb of God, who takes away the sin of the world!' (John 1:29). The ox and donkey, while not mentioned in Luke's narrative, derive from Isaiah 1:3: 'The ox knows its owner, and the donkey its master's crib, but Israel does not know, my people do not understand' — a poignant commentary on how even beasts recognize their Creator while his own people fail to. Joseph's kneeling posture reflects his obedient faith — the righteous man who, warned in a dream, took Mary as his wife and accepted the mystery of the virgin birth (Matthew 1:20-24). The scene radiates the paradox Paul celebrates: 'Though he was rich, yet for your sake he became poor, so that you by his poverty might become rich' (2 Corinthians 8:9).

scripture_refs.reason: The birth of Jesus in Bethlehem, wrapped in swaddling clothes and laid in a manger — the direct scene depicted
The lamb beside the infant foreshadows John the Baptist's declaration of Jesus as the Lamb of God
The ox and donkey recognizing their master's crib — the traditional source for animals at the nativity
Joseph's obedient acceptance of the virgin birth, depicted in his kneeling adoration
The prophecy of the ruler coming from Bethlehem whose origins are from ancient days
